text: This is a twenty-five-year-old man with acute severe ulcerative colitis, who received a subtotal colectomy emergently eight months ago. After surgery, the patient suffered with fever, ileus, and blood-stomach output. Endoscopy revealed deep ulcer in esophagus, stomach, duodenum, and small bowel. He was diagnosed with diffuse enteritis and response to steroid whale. However, The patient had bowel obstruction after two months, which was response poorly to conservative therapy.

During the laparotomy, the surgeon identifies severe adhesions and an interloop abscess near the stoma, along with two sealed perforations on the jejunum. The terminal ileum is resected approximately thirty centimeters, and the perforations are repaired. The visual findings are consistent with enteritis and ulcers.

After four months, the patient recovered well and gained weight of five kilogram. The timing had been established for completion, prostatectomy, and pouch creation. The key points are whether the surgery can be accomplished by a laparoscopic approach. and whether the small bowel is long enough to create the pouch and do pouch anastomosis.

The surgeon makes a circular incision around the terminal ileum and takes down the ileostomy. A 12mm trocar is inserted into the defect, and pneumoperitoneum is established for exploratory laparoscopy. Multiple adhesions between the small bowel, peritoneum, and rectal stump are visible and are released using an ultrasonic dissector.
The surgeon performs a mesorectal excision, sealing the rectal vessel separately using Hem-o-lok clips.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the rectum down to the levator muscles. A recto-digital examination is performed, revealing a very low dissection. The rectal stump, previously attached to the abdominal wall, is released.

The surgeon laparoscopically staples off the distal rectum using a GIA-Fortify device. The small bowel is assessed and deemed insufficient for pouch creation and IPAA. The rectum and ileum are grasped with instruments and exteriorized through a lower abdominal incision. Another firing of the GIA-Forty-Five stapler is used to remove the ileal segment leading towards the ileostomy.

The surgeon oversews the dead end of the J-pouch using a running suture, followed by a leak test that shows no leakage. The head of a 29mm circular stapler is inserted into the tip of the pouch, which is then reintroduced into the abdominal cavity. The shaft of the stapler is positioned into the short anal rectal stump, creating the ileal pouch-anal anastomosis without tension. A subsequent leak test confirms the absence of leaks.
The efferent lobe was averted and the idiostomy was matured. The patient received idiostomy reversal in July, and recovered well. The frequency of stool was four to seven times per day now.
